t: Hoarding clogs the living area and significantly disrupts purposeful use of rooms.
Significant distress or functional impairment (including dangerous environment to self, family, others).
Not better explained by another mental disorder (OCD 'obsessions', MDD 'lack of energy', psychotic disorder delusions, autism 'restricted interests').
DSM-5-TR / ICD-11 — separate category; insight qualifier.
"Animal hoarding" — although not a separate specifier, it is a clinically recognized variant; collaboration with veterinary and public health authorities is required.
Home visit or photographic evidence — condition of living space (Clutter Image Rating).
Comorbidity and cognitive assessment (dementia exclusion in elderly — MMSE/MoCA).
Classic obsessions (cleanliness, symmetry); hoarding items with emotional value.
Not cleaning due to "lack of energy"; no active acquisition of items.
Cognitive decline dominant; later developed accumulation.
Limited interests and attachment to objects; social-communicative deficit also present.
Self-care impairment + hoarding; social isolation; dementia comorbid is common.
Medical and cognitive examination (MMSE/MoCA for elderly).
CBT for hoarding (Frost R.O., Tolin D.F., Steketee G.)

— First-line; manualized 26-session protocol — motivation enhancement, cognitive restructuring, decision-making training, sorting and discarding exercises, home visits. Evidence: Tolin D.F. et al. Depress Anxiety 2015 RCT — medium-large effect.
SSRIs — evidence base is limited; SSRIs are less effective in OCD than in depression. As adjunctive.
Cognitive remediation — attention, categorization, decision-making training.
Buoyancy approach — supporting the patient's internal motivation; risk of recurrent blockage with forced cleaning.
Community intervention — public health, social service, fire safety.
In older adults — dementia screening and comorbid intervention.
Tolin D.F. et al. RCTs — CBT is effective; SSRIs limited.
The symptoms — are less severe than those observed in the USSR.
CBT for hoarding disorder (Hoarding) — Steketee (Steketee G.), Frost (Frost R.O.)
Manualized 26 sessions — motivational interview, decision-making training, sorting exercise, home visits, cognitive restructuring.
Hoarding Questionnaire-Revised (SI-R — Saving Inventory-Revised) — Frost (Frost R.O.)
23 items; three subscales (acquisition, difficulty discarding, clutter).
9-point scale; photo-based assessment of room clutter level.
Chronic course, severity increases with age.
Significant improvement with CBT, but full remission rare.
Myth 1: "Hoarding is just a 'collector' personality trait or laziness"

Collecting is a clinical disorder — Frost & Hartl cognitive-behavioral model; distinct neurobiology model; functional impairment and health risk. Unlike collectionism, items are not systematized and congest the living area.
Myth 2: "Forcibly cleaning the patient's home ends the problem"
Compulsive cleansing without the patient's consent leads to severe distress, trauma, and recurrence of obstruction within 12 months. Gradual sorting involving patient participation is recommended.
Myth 3: "Hoarding is a subtype of typical OCD; SSRIs are sufficient"
DSM-5 and ICD-11 — separate category; response to SSRIs is poor compared to OCD; CBT manualized protocol is first-line.
Myth 4: "Keeping many animals 'with good intentions' is good for the animals"

"Animal hoarding" — results in animals receiving poor hygiene, food, and medical care; high animal mortality, survivors in severe condition; requires intervention from veterinary and public health authorities.
Myth 5: "Hoarding in the elderly is 'normal aging'"
In elderly individuals, this may be a marker for frontotemporal dementia in particular; cognitive assessment is required.
Myth 6: "Once cleaned, the problem is solved"
Schizophrenia is a chronic disorder; long-term treatment and community support are required.
Frost R.O., Hartl T.L. A cognitive-behavioral model of compulsive hoarding. Behav Res Ther 1996;34(4):341–350.
Steketee G., Frost R.O. Treatment for Hoarding Disorder: Therapist Guide. Oxford Univ Press; 2014.
Tolin D.F., Frost R.O., Steketee G. et al. Cognitive behavioral therapy for hoarding disorder: a meta-analysis. Depress Anxiety 2015;32(3):158–166.
Samuels J.F. et al. Prevalence and correlates of hoarding behavior in a community-based sample. Behav Res Ther 2008;46(7):836–844.
